audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Ruth gleaning, Boaz's field, labor protections, shared meal, harvest provision, and redeemer recognition narrative, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for gibbor-hayil language, Ruth's initiative, chance/providence wording, gleaning rights, overseer report, foreigner language, Boaz's anti-harassment orders, wing imagery, meal inclusion, deliberate surplus, ephah quantity, Naomi's blessing, living/dead covenant-kindness, redeemer terminology, and harvest duration.

verses.restored: Naomi had a kinsman of her husband, a man of strength and standing, from clan of Elimelech, and his name was Boaz.
Ruth the Moabite said to Naomi, Let me go, please, to field and glean among ears of grain after one in whose eyes I may find favor. She said to her, Go, my daughter.
She went, came, and gleaned in field after reapers. Her chance happened upon portion of field belonging to Boaz, who was from clan of Elimelech.
Look, Boaz came from Bethlehem and said to reapers, YHWH be with you. They said to him, YHWH bless you.
Boaz said to his young man who stood over reapers, To whom does this young woman belong?
The young man who stood over reapers answered and said, She is the Moabite young woman who returned with Naomi from fields of Moab.
She said, Let me glean, please, and gather among sheaves after reapers. She came and has remained from morning until now; her sitting in shelter has been little.
Boaz said to Ruth, Have you not heard, my daughter? Do not go to glean in another field, and also do not pass away from this one, but cling here with my young women.
Let your eyes be on field they reap, and go after them. Have I not commanded young men not to touch you? When you thirst, go to vessels and drink from what young men draw.
She fell upon her face, bowed to ground, and said to him, Why have I found favor in your eyes, that you recognize me, though I am a foreign woman?

Boaz answered and said to her, It has been fully told to me all that you did for your mother-in-law after death of your husband, and how you left your father, your mother, and land of your birth and went to a people you had not known before.
May YHWH repay your work, and may your wages be full from YHWH, Divine of Israel, under whose wings you have come to seek refuge.
She said, May I find favor in your eyes, my lord, because you have comforted me and spoken to heart of your servant, though I am not like one of your female servants.
At mealtime Boaz said to her, Come here, eat from bread, and dip your morsel in sour wine. She sat beside reapers, and he handed her roasted grain. She ate, was satisfied, and had some left.
She arose to glean, and Boaz commanded his young men, saying, Even among sheaves let her glean, and do not humiliate her.
Also pull out some from bundles for her and leave it so she may glean, and do not rebuke her.
She gleaned in field until evening and beat out what she had gleaned; it was about an ephah of barley.
She lifted it and came to city, and her mother-in-law saw what she had gleaned. Ruth brought out and gave to her what she had left after being satisfied.

Her mother-in-law said to her, Where did you glean today, and where did you work? Blessed be one who recognized you. She told her mother-in-law with whom she had worked and said, Name of man with whom I worked today is Boaz.
Naomi said to her daughter-in-law, Blessed be he by YHWH, who has not abandoned his covenant-kindness with living and with dead. Naomi said to her, The man is near to us; he is one of our redeemers.
Ruth the Moabite said, He also said to me, Cling with my workers until they finish all harvest that is mine.
Naomi said to Ruth her daughter-in-law, It is good, my daughter, that you go out with his young women, so that in another field they do not assault or harass you.
She clung to young women of Boaz to glean until completion of barley harvest and wheat harvest, and she dwelt with her mother-in-law.

translation_notes: Gleaning is a survival right within Israel's land law, not merely private charity. Ruth nevertheless depends on landowners and workers honoring that right, exposing the vulnerability of poor and migrant laborers.
Verse 3 uses chance language: Ruth's 'chance happened' upon Boaz's field. The narrative can hold providence without denying contingency, labor, and human responsibility.
'To whom does this young woman belong?' locates Ruth within a household and labor system; it does not establish that Ruth is property.
Boaz's order that men not touch Ruth and Naomi's concern about assault show a real workplace danger. Basic safety, water, food, and freedom from harassment are obligations, not romantic favors.
Ruth calls herself a foreign woman. Boaz answers by recognizing her actions and migration story rather than erasing her Moabite identity.

Refuge under Divine wings is paired with concrete protections: safe work, water, inclusion at the meal, freedom from humiliation, and deliberate access to grain. Spiritual blessing cannot substitute for material justice.
Boaz holds economic, gendered, social, and local power over Ruth. The chapter must not romanticize a boss-worker imbalance or treat gratitude as consent to intimacy.
Ruth works from morning to evening and carries a substantial yield home. The text honors her labor; it does not depict poverty as picturesque or effortless.

Naomi's statement that covenant-kindness has not abandoned living and dead may refer to YHWH or Boaz; the Hebrew permits discussion. In either case, care becomes visible through provision and kinship responsibility.
Redeemer language concerns family land, continuity, and protection. It must not be converted into ownership of Ruth or a demand that vulnerable women be rescued by powerful men.
safety_note: Center Ruth as a migrant worker with initiative and rights. Do not romanticize poverty, workplace power imbalance, harassment risk, or dependence on a wealthy landholder. Divine-wing imagery must remain joined to food, water, safety, fair access, and freedom from humiliation.
